title: Quantum Limits of Electronic Transport in Nanostructured Macroscopic Conductors
authors: Agnieszka E. Lekawa-Raus, John S. Bulmer, Teresa Kulka, Magdalena Marganska, Nick Papior, Dwight G. Rickel, Fedor F. Balakirev, Jacek A. Majewski, Krzysztof Koziol, Karolina Z. Milowska
abstract: Macroscopic assemblies of one- and two-dimensional materials promise to translate nanoscale electronic properties into device-scale performance, yet the microscopic principles governing charge transport in such networks remain unresolved. In these systems, conductivity is often interpreted using phenomenological models that do not explicitly connect electronic structure to macroscopic magnetotransport. Here we develop a unified atomistic framework that links quantum-coherent transport, thermal disorder and magnetic-field effects, and combine it with ultrahigh-field magnetotransport measurements up to 60 T over a broad temperature range on carbon nanotube fibres. We show that positive magnetoresistance is controlled by junction overlap length, whereas negative magnetoresistance arises predominantly from lattice-mismatched heterojunctions rather than weak localisation alone. Statistical analysis of a large-scale numerical dataset reveals that the experimentally observed positive quadratic magnetoresistance originates from junction transport. These results show that macroscopic transport in disordered low-dimensional networks is governed primarily by junction-level quantum interference rather than solely by defects or doping.
summary: This paper provides a new atomistic framework to explain how quantum interference at junctions governs the macroscopic electrical properties of carbon nanotube networks. By combining theory with high-field measurements, the authors show that transport is driven by junction-level physics rather than simple defects or doping. This offers a way to bridge the gap between nanoscale electronic properties and device-scale performance.
main_problem: The microscopic principles governing charge transport in macroscopic assemblies of 1D and 2D materials remain unresolved, as current phenomenological models fail to connect electronic structure to macroscopic magnetotransport.
main_result: The study demonstrates that macroscopic transport in disordered low-dimensional networks is governed by junction-level quantum interference, where positive magnetoresistance is controlled by junction overlap length and negative magnetoresistance arises from lattice-mismatched heterojunctions.
method: The authors develop a unified atomistic framework linking quantum-coherent transport, thermal disorder, and magnetic-field effects, which is then validated against ultrahigh-field magnetotransport measurements up to 60 T.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of macroscopic assemblies of carbon nanotube fibres, treated as disordered networks of one- and two-dimensional materials.
key_observables: Magnetoresistance (positive and negative), conductivity, and junction transport properties.
important_parameters: Magnetic field strength (up to 60 T), temperature range, junction overlap length, and lattice mismatch.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of transport in macroscopic 1D/2D networks, presents a new atomistic theoretical framework, describes ultrahigh-field experimental measurements on carbon nanotube fibres, and concludes with a statistical analysis of the data to identify the origin of magnetoresistance.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
